analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

bottleneck.description: All 20 Kernel2 (cuBLAS GEMM) instances across the entire profile show tensor_core_active_pct=0.0. On Blackwell (SM100), FP32 GEMMs route through TF32 Tensor Cores only when torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32=True. The evidence strongly indicates this flag is False, forcing cuBLAS onto the FP32 SIMT path. The SIMT FP32 peak is roughly 8-16x slower than the TF32 or BF16 Tensor Core path on this hardware. Every mm node in the graph is suffering this deficit. The eligible_cycles_pct=58% confirms warps are issuing continuously to SIMT ALUs, not stalled — the bottleneck is instruction throughput on the wrong execution unit.

transformation.description: Two-stage fix. Stage 1 (zero graph change): set torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32=True before torch.compile, which instructs cuBLAS to use TF32 Tensor Cores for FP32 GEMMs. This is the minimum viable fix and requires no FX surgery. Stage 2 (full BF16 promotion via FX pass): insert aten.to.dtype casts to bfloat16 before every aten.mm.default node and cast outputs back to float32. BF16 unconditionally engages Tensor Cores regardless of the allow_tf32 flag and provides approximately 2x more throughput than TF32 on Blackwell. The FX pass operates on the post-Dynamo ATen IR graph passed to the Inductor backend.
transformation.location: Every torch.ops.aten.mm.default node in the graph (13 individually attributed + 8 in prologue partitions)
fx_steps:
# Stage 1 — set before torch.compile call (no graph change):
import torch
torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True

# Stage 2 — FX pass for full BF16 promotion:
import torch
import torch.fx as fx

def promote_mm_to_bf16(gm: fx.GraphModule) -> fx.GraphModule:
    graph = gm.graph
    for node in list(graph.nodes):
        if (node.op == 'call_function'
                and node.target == torch.ops.aten.mm.default):
            # Cast both inputs to bf16
            with graph.inserting_before(node):
                cast_a = graph.call_function(
                    torch.ops.aten.to.dtype,
                    args=(node.args[0], torch.bfloat16)
                )
                cast_b = graph.call_function(
                    torch.ops.aten.to.dtype,
                    args=(node.args[1], torch.bfloat16)
                )
            node.args = (cast_a, cast_b)
            # Cast output back to fp32 for downstream compatibility
            with graph.inserting_after(node):
                cast_out = graph.call_function(
                    torch.ops.aten.to.dtype,
                    args=(node, torch.float32)
                )
            node.replace_all_uses_with(cast_out)
            cast_out.args = (node,) + cast_out.args[1:]
    graph.lint()
    gm.recompile()
    return gm

# Register as a post-grad Inductor pass:
from torch._inductor import config as inductor_config
inductor_config.post_grad_custom_pre_pass = promote_mm_to_bf16
transformation.code_hint:
torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True  # minimum fix
# or: model = model.bfloat16(); inputs = inputs.bfloat16()  # full fix

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: None directly — same DRAM traffic, but Tensor Core throughput increases 8-16x so wall time drops substantially. With TF32: ~2x GEMM speedup, saving ~611K ns. With BF16: ~4x speedup, saving ~917K ns.

optimizations.notes: The kernel name 'Kernel2' with 210 registers/thread and 49.15 KB dynamic shared memory is characteristic of a cuBLAS GEMM algorithm (not a Triton kernel). Triton-generated GEMM kernels for this shape would have different register profiles. The allow_tf32 flag is the most likely root cause. Assumption: model was compiled with default PyTorch settings where allow_tf32 may be True globally but was overridden at the workload level (e.g., via torch.set_default_dtype(torch.float32) in a context that also disables TF32). The fused_kernel_double_count flag is active: the prologue's 8 Kernel2 instances and the individually attributed mm operators represent non-overlapping kernel IDs, so the total duration sum (1,223,093 ns) is not double-counted.

bottleneck.description: Three sequential identical-shape GEMM kernels (Q, K, V projections) are launched back-to-back per attention layer: each computes [4096,512] x [512,512] with tensor_core_active_pct=0. Beyond the dtype bottleneck (addressed in OPT-1), each launches a separate kernel with a 1-wave grid (128 CTAs on ~170 SMs = 75% SM utilization). Three independent launches cannot pipeline and each suffers the wave tail. Fusing to a single [4096,512] x [512,1536] GEMM increases N from 512 to 1536, moving the grid from [64,1,2]=128 CTAs to approximately [64,3,2]=384 CTAs (2-3 waves), improving SM utilization and enabling better L2 data reuse of the shared input matrix X across all three projections.

transformation.description: Pre-concatenate the Q, K, V weight matrices (each [512,512]) into a single [512,1536] parameter. Replace the three sequential aten.mm.default nodes that share the same input activation with one aten.mm.default call against the concatenated weight, then split the [4096,1536] output with aten.split.Tensor into three [4096,512] tensors. This eliminates 2 kernel launches per attention layer (4 launches across 2 identified triplets, plus additional savings if the prologue contains equivalent triplets). Weight concatenation is done once at model initialization via register_buffer, not at each forward pass.
transformation.location: aten::mm triplets (op_ids 4+5+6 and op_ids 26+27+28) where args[0] is the same input activation and args[1] shapes are all [512, 512]
fx_steps:
# Identify QKV triplets: mm nodes sharing the same first argument
from collections import defaultdict
import torch
import torch.fx as fx

def fuse_qkv_projections(gm: fx.GraphModule) -> fx.GraphModule:
    graph = gm.graph
    mm_nodes = [
        n for n in graph.nodes
        if n.op == 'call_function'
        and n.target == torch.ops.aten.mm.default
    ]
    # Group consecutive mm nodes by shared input (args[0])
    by_input: dict = defaultdict(list)
    for n in mm_nodes:
        by_input[n.args[0]].append(n)

    for shared_input, group in by_input.items():
        if len(group) < 3:
            continue
        q_node, k_node, v_node = group[0], group[1], group[2]
        w_q, w_k, w_v = q_node.args[1], k_node.args[1], v_node.args[1]

        # Pre-concat weights along dim=1: [512,512]*3 -> [512,1536]
        with graph.inserting_before(q_node):
            cat_w = graph.call_function(
                torch.ops.aten.cat.default,
                args=([w_q, w_k, w_v],),
                kwargs={'dim': 1}
            )
            fused = graph.call_function(
                torch.ops.aten.mm.default,
                args=(shared_input, cat_w)
            )
            # Split [4096,1536] -> three [4096,512]
            splits = graph.call_function(
                torch.ops.aten.split.Tensor,
                args=(fused, 512, 1)
            )
            q_out = graph.call_function(
                operator.getitem, args=(splits, 0)
            )
            k_out = graph.call_function(
                operator.getitem, args=(splits, 1)
            )
            v_out = graph.call_function(
                operator.getitem, args=(splits, 2)
            )
        q_node.replace_all_uses_with(q_out)
        k_node.replace_all_uses_with(k_out)
        v_node.replace_all_uses_with(v_out)

    graph.eliminate_dead_code()
    graph.lint()
    gm.recompile()
    return gm
transformation.code_hint:
# In module __init__, pre-concatenate and register:
self.register_buffer('qkv_weight',
    torch.cat([self.q_proj.weight, self.k_proj.weight, self.v_proj.weight], dim=0))
# In forward: Q, K, V = F.linear(x, self.qkv_weight).chunk(3, dim=-1)

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Input activation X ([4096,512]) is loaded from L2 once for a single fused GEMM instead of three times for three independent GEMMs. L2 reuse reduces effective DRAM reads by approximately 2/3 for the input tensor per triplet.

optimizations.notes: This transformation assumes the three mm nodes sharing input X are topologically adjacent with no intervening writes to X, which is standard for self-attention QKV projections. The weight cat is done at graph instantiation (or module init), not in the forward pass, so the cat node itself adds no runtime cost. Apply OPT-1 first so the fused GEMM also benefits from Tensor Cores. Impact estimate is for the 2 identified named triplets (6 kernels × ~61K ns); additional savings accrue from equivalent triplets inside the prologue partition (estimated 4 more triplet executions).

bottleneck.description: The attention kernel 'fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80' is a CUTLASS FlashAttention variant compiled for SM80 (Ampere) running on a Blackwell SM100 GPU. Two distinct problems: (1) The kernel was compiled for SM80 and cannot use SM100 wgmma (warp-group matrix multiply-accumulate) instructions introduced in Hopper/Blackwell; it falls back to SM80 mma instructions, which are slower on Blackwell's pipeline. (2) The kernel has 168 registers/thread, causing 757,760 local memory spill transactions per launch. At the fmha grid of 512 CTAs, these spills add significant DRAM traffic beyond the theoretical attention IO. Achieved occupancy is only 14% because the 168-register kernel can only fit 1-2 blocks per SM. Switching to BF16 inputs would cause PyTorch to dispatch to a Blackwell-native cuDNN or Flash Attention 3 kernel with SM100 ISA instructions and better register allocation.

transformation.description: After OPT-1 promotes mm inputs to BF16, ensure the Q/K/V tensors entering scaled_dot_product_attention are also BF16. PyTorch 2.11 on Blackwell will then dispatch to the Flash Attention 3 backend (via cuDNN attention or the torch.backends.cuda.flash_sdp path), which is compiled for SM100 and uses wgmma instructions. Additionally, explicitly enable the Flash SDP backend and disable the memory-efficient SDP backend to prevent fallback to the SM80 CUTLASS kernel. No FX node surgery to the _efficient_attention_forward node itself is needed; the backend selection is transparent once dtype is correct.
transformation.location: torch.ops.aten._scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention.default (3 nodes) — the outer aten::_efficient_attention_forward nodes are their dispatch targets
fx_steps:
# Set backend preferences before torch.compile:
import torch
torch.backends.cuda.enable_flash_sdp(True)
torch.backends.cuda.enable_mem_efficient_sdp(False)
torch.backends.cuda.enable_math_sdp(False)

# If FX surgery is needed to force the Flash path explicitly,
# replace aten._scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention with
# aten._scaled_dot_product_flash_attention:
import torch
import torch.fx as fx

def replace_efficient_attn_with_flash(gm: fx.GraphModule) -> fx.GraphModule:
    graph = gm.graph
    target = torch.ops.aten._scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention.default
    flash_target = torch.ops.aten._scaled_dot_product_flash_attention.default
    for node in list(graph.nodes):
        if node.op == 'call_function' and node.target == target:
            # Flash attention signature: (query, key, value, attn_mask,
            #   dropout_p, is_causal, scale)
            # Efficient attention signature: (query, key, value,
            #   attn_bias, compute_log_sumexp, dropout_p, is_causal, scale)
            q, k, v = node.args[0], node.args[1], node.args[2]
            is_causal = node.kwargs.get('is_causal', False)
            scale = node.kwargs.get('scale', None)
            with graph.inserting_before(node):
                new_node = graph.call_function(
                    flash_target,
                    args=(q, k, v),
                    kwargs={
                        'dropout_p': 0.0,
                        'is_causal': is_causal,
                        'scale': scale,
                        'return_debug_mask': False
                    }
                )
            # Flash returns (output, logsumexp, cum_seq_q, cum_seq_k,
            #   max_q, max_k, philox_seed, philox_offset, debug_mask)
            # Efficient returns (output, logsumexp, philox_seed, philox_offset)
            # Replace only the output (index 0) uses
            for user in list(node.users):
                if (user.op == 'call_function'
                        and user.target == operator.getitem
                        and user.args[1] == 0):
                    with graph.inserting_before(user):
                        flash_out = graph.call_function(
                            operator.getitem, args=(new_node, 0)
                        )
                    user.replace_all_uses_with(flash_out)
            if not node.users:
                graph.erase_node(node)
    graph.lint()
    gm.recompile()
    return gm

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Eliminating 757,760 local memory spill transactions per kernel launch (3 named + 2 prologue = 5 launches) reduces DRAM traffic from register spills by approximately 5 x 757,760 x 32 bytes = ~121 MB equivalent in spill traffic. Additionally, SM100 wgmma allows higher occupancy (estimated 25-30% vs current 14%), enabling more latency hiding.

optimizations.notes: Confidence is medium because Flash Attention 3 availability on PyTorch 2.11 for Blackwell depends on the specific cuDNN version bundled with the CUDA 12.8 install. If cuDNN >= 9.0 is present, torch.backends.cuda.enable_flash_sdp will dispatch to the cuDNN-based attention kernel which is SM100-native. If not, the fallback remains the SM80 CUTLASS kernel. Verify with torch.backends.cuda.flash_sdp_enabled(). The FX surgery step is a fallback if the backend flag approach fails. OPT-1 is prerequisite because the SM80 kernel is the only available choice for FP32 inputs on the currently detected runtime; BF16 is needed to unlock the alternative backends.

bottleneck.description: Each attention output projection (mm followed by residual add) launches two separate kernels: one Kernel2 GEMM and one elementwise add. The add is not fused with the GEMM because aten::mm returns a new tensor and aten::add follows as a separate node. cuBLAS exposes aten::addmm (C = alpha*A@B + beta*C) which computes GEMM + add in a single kernel call, eliminating the separate elementwise kernel and reading the residual tensor only once. Each eliminated add kernel reduces memory bandwidth: the residual tensor ([4096,512] fp32 = 8 MB) is read once in addmm vs once for add separately. With 3 attention layers the savings are 3 avoided kernel launches and 3 fewer full-tensor reads.

evidence.kernel: Kernel2 + downstream elementwise kernel (not separately profiled but implied by graph structure)

transformation.description: Walk the ATen graph and find patterns where aten.mm.default is the sole producer consumed by aten.add.Tensor. Replace the (mm, add) pair with aten.addmm.default(residual, A, B), which maps directly to cuBLAS GEMM+bias in a single call. The residual tensor becomes the C matrix in the BLAS call. This transformation is safe only when the mm node has exactly one user (the add node) and the add output shape matches the mm output shape.
transformation.location: aten.mm.default nodes (op_ids 21 and 43 output projections, and one or more within aten::mm call_index=0) where the sole user is aten.add.Tensor with a residual connection
fx_steps:
import torch
import torch.fx as fx

def fuse_mm_add_to_addmm(gm: fx.GraphModule) -> fx.GraphModule:
    graph = gm.graph
    for node in list(graph.nodes):
        if (node.op != 'call_function'
                or node.target != torch.ops.aten.mm.default):
            continue
        users = list(node.users)
        if len(users) != 1:
            continue
        add_node = users[0]
        if (add_node.op != 'call_function'
                or add_node.target not in (
                    torch.ops.aten.add.Tensor,
                    torch.ops.aten.add_.Tensor
                )):
            continue
        # Determine which add argument is the residual
        a_mat, b_mat = node.args[0], node.args[1]
        if add_node.args[0] is node:
            residual = add_node.args[1]
        else:
            residual = add_node.args[0]
        # Replace mm + add with addmm(residual, A, B)
        with graph.inserting_before(node):
            addmm_node = graph.call_function(
                torch.ops.aten.addmm.default,
                args=(residual, a_mat, b_mat)
            )
        add_node.replace_all_uses_with(addmm_node)
        graph.erase_node(add_node)
        if not node.users:
            graph.erase_node(node)
    graph.eliminate_dead_code()
    graph.lint()
    gm.recompile()
    return gm
transformation.code_hint:
# Inductor often does this automatically; verify it isn't already fused
# by checking if 'addmm' appears in the compiled graph:
# torch.compile(model, backend='inductor').__wrapped__.print_readable()

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: 3 fewer reads of the [4096,512] fp32 residual tensor (~8 MB each), saving ~24 MB of DRAM reads across 3 output projections. The elementwise add kernels for a tensor this size are fast (<5 µs each) so primary benefit is launch overhead reduction and L2 pressure relief.

optimizations.notes: Confidence is medium because Inductor may already perform mm+add -> addmm folding internally as part of its epilogue fusion. Verify by inspecting the compiled kernel count: if the number of elementwise kernels after the output projection mm is zero, this optimization is already applied. If Inductor is already fusing, this FX pass is a no-op and harmless. The impact estimate is conservative because the add kernels are not separately profiled in this trace; only the mm durations are measured. The fused_kernel_double_count edge case flag means the prologue operator may contain additional mm+add pairs not visible as individual named operators.

global_notes: 2 operators below 1% threshold omitted (combined 1.10% of attributed time): aten::_unsafe_view (0.62%, fused with add+native_layer_norm) and aten::native_layer_norm (0.48%)

Device is NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (SM100+). warp_cycles_per_instruction is unavailable on Blackwell — using eligible_cycles_pct < 20 as the latency-bound indicator throughout. All warp_cycles_per_instruction fields in the profile are null, confirming Blackwell architecture.

SM count assumed ~170 (Blackwell GB202-class die). Kernel2 grid [64,1,2]=128 CTAs yields ceil(128/170)=1 wave — the GEMM saturates only 75% of SMs per wave. fmha grid [8,8,8]=512 CTAs yields ceil(512/170)=4 waves.

fused_kernel_double_count flag active: layer::unique::prologue (is_fused=true) and both named aten::_efficient_attention_forward (op_ids 14 and 36) and aten::_unsafe_view (is_fused=true) operators contain kernels that were also individually profiled. Kernel IDs across operator buckets are non-overlapping (verified by inspection), so total attributed duration (1,838,671 ns) does not double-count at the kernel level.

The model executes 3 full attention layers: Layer 1 attributed via high-confidence torch_profiler to aten::mm (call_index=0) + aten::_efficient_attention_forward (call_index=0). Layers 2 and 3 attributed via NVTX to the mm op_id=4/5/6/21 and op_id=26/27/28/43 groups plus their respective _efficient_attention_forward calls. The prologue NVTX partition contains replayed instances of the same kernel types, consistent with layer deduplication.

GEMM shapes are [4096,512]x[512,512]: M=4096 (batch=8 x seq=512), N=512 (d_model), K=512 (d_model). These are square-ish GEMMs that should tile efficiently with Tensor Cores if the dtype path is corrected. Arithmetic intensity ≈ 2x4096x512x512 / (2x4096x512 + 512x512 + 4096x512) bytes ≈ 170 FLOP/byte, well above the Blackwell ridge point, meaning these GEMMs are compute-bound and will benefit maximally from Tensor Core engagement.

OPT-1 (dtype/TF32) is the highest-leverage change. All other optimizations deliver incremental improvement on top of it. Recommended application order: OPT-1 first, verify Tensor Core engagement (tensor_core_active_pct should rise to >50%), then apply OPT-2 and OPT-3 in parallel, then OPT-4 last.
